vllm-cpu.content: What's the status of the research phase?

I've generated 8 research leads from your IdeaBot answers. These leads cover the vLLM upstream ecosystem, CPU optimization capabilities (AVX-512, AMX), Red Hat Inference Server integration points, and strategic market positioning. Ready to execute research when you are!
How long will the research phase take?

Research typically takes 30-60 minutes. I'll analyze GitHub activity, benchmark CPU vs GPU performance, review RHIS architecture docs, and scan for security issues in parallel. You'll get comprehensive findings across all 5 research vectors.

ideabot.default: I can help you refine your project idea. What specific aspect would you like to discuss?
ideabot.strategic priority: OCTO's current strategic priorities include: AI Inference Acceleration, Edge Computing, Cloud Native Observability, and Secure Software Supply Chain. Your idea should align with one of these areas to maximize impact and ensure catcher team availability.
ideabot.catcher: The catcher team is the downstream product engineering team that will receive the prototype after OCTO completes development. It's critical to identify and engage them early - they need to be willing to take ownership of the technology for long-term support and productization. I can help you identify the right catcher based on your idea's focus area.
ideabot.transfer: The transfer process follows our Tech Alignment Agreement template. Key steps: (1) Initial planning with catcher team, (2) Knowledge transfer sessions, (3) Repository ownership handoff, (4) Retrospective. The goal is to ensure the catcher team is set up for success, not just 'throwing it over the fence.'
ideabot.timeline: Typical OCTO projects run 3-6 months from idea to transfer. The timeline depends on technical complexity and catcher team availability. We aim to move quickly while ensuring quality and proper knowledge transfer.
ideabot.help: I evaluate ideas based on: market relevance, strategic alignment, technical feasibility, catcher team buy-in, and resource availability. Answer the 11 questions thoroughly, and I'll provide a recommendation on whether to proceed with ProtoBot prototyping.

blueprint.default: I'm conducting research across five vectors to build a comprehensive technical blueprint. What specific area would you like to explore?
blueprint.research: My research methodology involves: (1) Upstream ecosystem analysis - GitHub activity, community health, license compatibility, (2) Strategic longevity - market trends, vendor roadmaps, industry adoption, (3) Product fit - integration with Red Hat portfolio, customer needs, (4) Security posture - CVE analysis, supply chain security, compliance requirements, (5) Technical constraints - performance characteristics, hardware requirements, architectural limitations.
blueprint.risks: I identify risks across all five research vectors and propose mitigations. Risks are color-coded: red for blockers requiring HIL decision, orange for manageable risks with clear mitigations, yellow for items to monitor. The goal is to surface issues early so we can address them proactively.
blueprint.security: Security research includes: CVE database scanning for the upstream project and dependencies, supply chain analysis (SBOM generation), compliance requirement mapping (FedRAMP, SOC2, etc.), container security best practices (UBI base images, non-root users, minimal attack surface), and Red Hat security review process alignment.
blueprint.upstream: Upstream community health is critical for long-term success. I evaluate: GitHub activity (commits, PRs, issues), maintainer responsiveness, contributor diversity (avoiding single-company dependency), license compatibility with Red Hat's open source strategy, and contribution acceptance rate. Strong upstream communities reduce maintenance burden and increase innovation velocity.
blueprint.community: Community strength indicators I track: number of active contributors, commit frequency, issue resolution time, presence of governance model, diversity of contributing organizations, conference presence, and ecosystem integrations. A healthy community provides confidence in long-term viability.
blueprint.findings: Research findings are organized by the five vectors. Each finding includes: the specific data point, source/citation, relevance to the project, and implications for design decisions. Findings inform the technical blueprint and help identify architectural constraints early.

orchestrator.default: I coordinate the execution agents (Code, Infrastructure, Operations) to generate your prototype artifacts. What would you like to know about the process?
orchestrator.agents: The execution model uses five specialized agents: (1) Blueprint Agent - conducts research and generates technical blueprint, (2) Code Generation Agent - writes source code, tests, build scripts, (3) Infrastructure Agent - creates Containerfiles, K8s manifests, CI/CD pipelines, (4) Operations Agent - generates documentation, emails, blog posts, (5) Orchestrator (me) - coordinates execution, validates outputs, handles failures.
orchestrator.coordination: I manage agent execution in waves: Wave 1 runs Code + Operations agents in parallel (no dependencies). Wave 2 runs Infrastructure agent after Code completes (needs dependency manifests). This parallel execution optimizes for speed while respecting dependencies. I cross-validate outputs between agents to ensure consistency.
orchestrator.validation: Cross-validation checks include: container image references match between Code and Infra, entry points are consistent, runtime dependencies align, hardware targeting is coherent, port mappings match, environment variables are synchronized, and security policies are compatible. Any mismatches are automatically resolved or escalated to HIL.
orchestrator.dependencies: Agent dependencies: Blueprint Agent runs first (provides context for all others). Code and Ops agents run in parallel (independent). Infrastructure agent depends on Code (needs requirements.txt, entrypoints). Orchestrator validates after all agents complete. This dependency model ensures efficient execution while maintaining correctness.
orchestrator.failures: When an agent fails, I first attempt autonomous recovery: retry with adjusted parameters, consult other agents for context, check for transient issues. If recovery fails after 3 attempts, I escalate to HIL with: failure description, attempted mitigations, recommended next steps. The goal is to handle recoverable failures without human intervention.
orchestrator.wave: Execution waves optimize for parallelism while respecting dependencies. Wave 1 (parallel): Code Generation + Operations. These agents have no interdependencies and can run simultaneously. Wave 2 (sequential): Infrastructure Agent runs after Code completes, using generated dependency manifests to build containers. This approach minimizes total execution time.
orchestrator.help: I orchestrate the prototype generation end-to-end. My responsibilities: manage agent execution order, cross-validate outputs, handle failures autonomously, report progress to HIL, ensure all artifacts are generated correctly. I aim to produce production-quality prototypes with minimal human intervention.
